description: Baseline benchmark harness found core/reference coverage but normalization was incorrect (>100 score), so discard this run and fix the metric scaling before comparing skill changes.

asi.hypothesis: Establish a representative jj-skill quality benchmark before editing the skill.
asi.rollback_reason: Benchmark normalization bug made the metric invalid for comparisons.
asi.next_action_hint: Fix the benchmark max-score calculation, rerun the baseline, then iterate on the jj skill and reference docs.

description: Corrected the jj-skill benchmark to score representative workflow coverage and established the baseline before editing the skill.

asi.hypothesis: A scenario-based benchmark with publish, recovery, and safety checks will expose real improvement headroom for the jj skill.
asi.notes: Current gaps are restore-safety carryover and diagnostic fetch guidance.
asi.next_action_hint: Improve the jj skill/reference with explicit restore safeguards, safer publish guidance, and diagnostic fetch/recovery instructions while keeping the top-level skill concise.

description: Improved the jj skill, reference, and Codex metadata with a clearer task triage, targeted inspection helpers, safer bookmark publish guidance, explicit quiet-fetch diagnostics, and repo-aligned restore/rewrite safety rules; benchmark rose from 83.87 to 100 and chezmoi diff/apply/status completed.

asi.hypothesis: Adding concise but concrete publish/recovery/safety guidance to the jj skill will improve real jj task handling without bloating the top-level skill.
asi.validation: chezmoi diff && chezmoi apply && chezmoi status
asi.learnings: The biggest missing capabilities were targeted change inspection, bookmark-publish checklist detail, quiet-fetch diagnostic guidance, and carrying AGENTS restore-safety rules into the jj docs.
asi.next_action_hint: Look for broader generalization opportunities beyond documentation wording, such as adding repo-specific jj examples or adjacent skill/router improvements if future jj tasks still stumble.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (held-out workflow rubric)

description: Started a held-out workflow rubric to avoid overfitting the first benchmark; current jj docs still under-specify the exact steps for publishing the current change/bookmark.

asi.hypothesis: A second benchmark focused on realistic publish/reshape workflows will reveal remaining jj-skill gaps after the first rubric maxed out.
asi.notes: Main remaining gap is precise current-change publishing guidance around bookmark move/create and push.
asi.next_action_hint: Tighten the bookmark publishing examples and mixed-task routing while keeping the top-level skill concise.

description: Refined the jj publish/recovery guidance again using a held-out workflow rubric: the reference now documents exact bookmark create/move-to-@/push steps for publishing the current change, and the top-level skill makes the immediate `jj undo` recovery path explicit; held-out score improved from 82 to 100 and chezmoi apply succeeded.

asi.hypothesis: Adding exact publish-current-change commands and a clearer fast-recovery cue will improve real jj task execution beyond the first benchmark.
asi.validation: chezmoi apply; checked git status afterward
asi.learnings: The first rubric overfit documentation breadth; a held-out workflow rubric exposed that command precision for bookmark publishing still mattered.
asi.next_action_hint: Consider a final pass for mixed-task routing or examples only if future jj tasks reveal confusion not already covered by the current docs.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (repo helper discoverability)

description: Started a repo-helper discoverability rubric to cover real jj ergonomics beyond core workflow docs; the jj reference currently omits the interactive jji/jjbi/jjfi helpers and shell aliases.

asi.hypothesis: Documenting the repo's interactive jj helpers and aliases should improve day-to-day jj operations beyond generic command reference coverage.
asi.notes: Large gap: repo-specific interactive helpers exist but are undocumented in the jj reference.
asi.next_action_hint: Add a compact helper section that explains jji, jjbi, jjfi, and the main shell aliases while preserving restore-safety guidance.

description: Extended the jj reference with repo-specific shell aliases and the interactive `jji`, `jjbi`, and `jjfi` helpers, including the key bindings and restore-safety note for `jjfi`; helper-discoverability score improved from 36 to 100 and chezmoi apply succeeded.

asi.hypothesis: Documenting the repo's jj aliases and interactive fzf helpers will improve real jj ergonomics, not just generic command recall.
asi.validation: chezmoi apply; checked git status afterward
asi.learnings: The repo already shipped powerful jj helper surfaces, but the jj skill reference did not surface them at all, so discoverability was the main gap.
asi.next_action_hint: If more jj iteration is needed, look for gaps in cross-linking from the top-level skill or other docs rather than adding more low-value detail to the reference.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (discovery surfaces)

description: Started a discovery-surface rubric for the jj skill; the remaining gap is that the top-level skill still points to `jj show`/revsets but not to the newly documented interactive jj helpers.

asi.hypothesis: After the reference is strong, improving the top-level skill and metadata discovery surfaces can further improve real jj usage by making the right affordances easier to find.
asi.notes: Current top-level workflow does not mention jji/jjbi/jjfi or helper-driven inspection paths.
asi.next_action_hint: Tighten the inspection-only path and reference blurb so the top-level skill explicitly points users toward helper-based inspection when appropriate.

description: Updated the top-level jj skill so the inspection path now explicitly points to the interactive `jji`/`jjbi`/`jjfi` helpers, and the reference blurb now advertises interactive helpers alongside aliases/recovery; discovery-surface score improved from 82 to 100 and chezmoi apply succeeded.

asi.hypothesis: Making the top-level skill explicitly route users to the interactive helper surfaces will improve jj usage without adding much verbosity.
asi.validation: chezmoi apply; checked git status afterward
asi.learnings: Once the reference was strong, the main remaining problem was discoverability from the top-level skill, not missing reference content.
asi.next_action_hint: If another jj experiment is needed, focus on adjacent docs that route users into the skill rather than expanding the skill body further.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (common recipes)

description: Tried a recipe-focused rubric for the jj reference, but the checks were too loose and existing sections satisfied them without requiring any new improvements, so discard this benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: A common-recipes rubric could expose copy-paste workflow gaps in the jj reference.
asi.rollback_reason: The regex checks matched existing prose and did not create useful optimization headroom.
asi.next_action_hint: Tighten future benchmarks around truly missing routing/docs surfaces or stop adding new workloads unless they reveal concrete gaps.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (routing docs)

description: Started a routing-docs rubric and found that `docs/tooling-and-skills.md` still undersells the richer jj skill surface even though the skill/reference themselves have improved.

asi.hypothesis: Improving the docs that route agents into the jj skill will help the stronger skill content get used in practice.
asi.notes: Main gap is the short jj bullet in docs/tooling-and-skills.md.
asi.next_action_hint: Update the shared tooling docs to mention bookmarks, recovery, and interactive helpers in the jj skill summary while keeping the list concise.

description: Updated `docs/tooling-and-skills.md` so the jj skill summary now advertises bookmark-safe publishing, recovery, and repo interactive helpers, improving routing into the richer jj skill surface; routing-doc score improved from 65 to 100 and chezmoi apply succeeded.

asi.hypothesis: Improving the docs that route agents into the jj skill will help the stronger skill content get used in practice.
asi.validation: chezmoi apply; checked git status afterward
asi.learnings: After strengthening the skill itself, the next limiting factor was discoverability from shared routing docs, not missing jj reference content.
asi.next_action_hint: Further jj work should target only concrete gaps found in real use; the core skill, reference, metadata, and routing docs are now aligned.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (real task discovery + routing)

description: Strengthened jj skill discovery surfaces by expanding the jj skill frontmatter and Codex metadata to mention recovery, safe publish flows, and repo interactive helpers; real-task discovery/routing score improved from 82 to 100 and chezmoi apply succeeded.

asi.hypothesis: Richer discovery metadata will make the improved jj guidance easier to trigger for real prompts like bad rebases, bookmark publishing, and helper-driven inspection.
asi.validation: chezmoi apply; checked git status afterward
asi.learnings: The remaining gap was not reference content but discovery metadata: the jj reference was strong, but the skill description and metadata under-advertised recovery and helper workflows.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune stale ideas, then if more exploration is useful, try a held-out routing benchmark across CLAUDE and other entry docs or build a higher-fidelity prompt-to-skill eval harness.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (held-out entry docs)

description: Updated `CLAUDE.md` so the jj routing text and `/jj` skill table row now mention recovery and interactive helpers, improving held-out jj entry-doc coverage from 84 to 100; chezmoi apply succeeded.

asi.hypothesis: Strengthening the main agent entry doc will make the enriched jj skill easier to trigger for real requests involving recovery and helper-driven workflows.
asi.validation: chezmoi apply; checked git status afterward
asi.learnings: After improving the skill, reference, metadata, and tooling docs, the remaining routing gap was the concise `/jj` summary in CLAUDE.md.
asi.next_action_hint: If more work is needed, build a higher-fidelity prompt-to-skill eval harness rather than adding more regex-only rubrics.

name: Improve jj skill guidance quality (prompt-family eval)

description: Tried a prompt-family eval harness, but the current jj docs already fully satisfied these representative prompt families, so this benchmark did not expose new improvement headroom.

asi.hypothesis: A prompt-family eval would reveal whether the improved jj docs still miss common request shapes like recovery, bookmark publishing, helper usage, or stacked-change work.
asi.rollback_reason: The benchmark provided no new headroom because the current skill stack already covered all tested prompt families.
asi.next_action_hint: Avoid adding more text-match-only rubrics; future work should use a higher-fidelity task eval or stop until real jj usage reveals new gaps.

description: Baseline warm `chezmoi apply` run with current scripts; a no-op apply still spends time in post-apply summary and health-check logic.

Skip the expensive full post-apply health check on routine warm applies unless VERBOSE, CHEZMOI_RUN_HEALTH_CHECK=1, or CHEZMOI_FORCE_UPDATE=1; also avoid sourcing NVM in the summary unless verbose output actually needs tool versions.

Confirmed the warm-apply win after documenting deferred follow-ups: routine applies still skip the heavy health audit by default, and the summary now avoids loading NVM unless verbose output needs tool versions.

Added cheap state-gated fast paths to Node and pi-maintenance-agent setup so routine warm applies skip sourcing NVM, re-resolving npm, and re-running systemd timer enablement when the managed links/install state are already converged; also pruned stale jj ideas from the backlog.

Added the same cheap state-gated fast path to Claude Code setup so routine warm applies trust the pinned state marker plus managed binary presence instead of paying Node CLI startup just to re-check an unchanged install; verbose apply still runs the health audit.

Tried the same state-gated fast path for uv by trusting the managed uv/python links instead of re-checking `uv --version` and `uv python list`, but warm `chezmoi apply` stayed at 0.7s so the change did not move the primary metric enough to justify keeping.

Tried extending the cheap state-gated fast path to jj, codex, bun, starship, and tailscale, but the warm-apply benchmark remained pinned at 0.7s, suggesting these smaller script wins are below the current single-run timing resolution.

Established a higher-fidelity batch benchmark of five consecutive warm `chezmoi apply` runs because the single-run benchmark had reached a 0.1s timing floor and could no longer resolve smaller script-level improvements.

Extended the cheap state-gated fast path to more version-pinned setup scripts (uv, jj, codex, bun, starship, and exact-version tailscale) so batch warm applies trust converged state markers plus managed binaries instead of re-running version probes every time; five warm applies improved from 3.7s to 3.5s and verbose apply still exercised the health audit.

Tried two warm-apply simplifications together: source helpers directly in the run-before scripts when already applied, and convert asset prefetch to `run_onchange_before_` with a versioned state key so pin bumps invalidate cleanly. The stability story improved conceptually, but the five-apply benchmark stayed at 3.5s, so discard this batch for the performance-targeted loop.

Established a functional stability harness for prefetch state invalidation by rendering the prefetch script, mutating a pinned version variable, and checking whether `PREFETCH_STATE` changes; the current static state key scores 0 because version bumps would reuse stale prefetch state.

Versioned the prefetch state key with the pinned artifact versions so pin bumps invalidate stale `prefetch-assets.done` markers, and pruned the stale Claude/prefetch ideas now that both are addressed; the functional invalidation harness improved from 0 to 100 and `chezmoi apply` still succeeds.
Established a more sensitive 20x warm-apply benchmark so smaller process-count simplifications can be measured after the 5x benchmark plateaued around 3.5s.

Extracted the full environment audit out of apply-time into a new on-demand `chezmoi-health-check` command, removed the always-launched post-apply health-check script, and updated the summary tip accordingly; 20 warm applies improved from 14.7s to 14.3s while both `chezmoi apply` and `chezmoi-health-check` still work.

Converted the optional asset prefetch script to `run_onchange_before_` now that its state key is versioned, so routine warm applies no longer launch a no-op prefetch process on every run; 20 warm applies improved from 14.7s to 13.9s and the architecture doc now matches the new script name.

Converted the Oh My Zsh custom-directory bootstrap to `run_onchange_before_` and recorded follow-up ideas for future process-count reductions; the script is a one-time directory scaffold, so routine warm applies no longer launch it every time, improving the 20x benchmark from 14.7s to 13.7s.

Converted the login-shell bootstrap to `run_onchange_after_` so routine warm applies stop re-running the shell-probe/chsh no-op path on every run, and pruned the stale follow-up idea now that this conversion is done; the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 14.7s to 13.2s.

Converted the tmux bootstrap to `run_onchange_after_` so routine warm applies no longer launch the no-op TPM/bootstrap script every time, and recorded a follow-up idea to document how to rerun these bootstrap-only scripts if users ever need to recover from manual deletions; the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 14.7s to 12.9s.

name: Tmux setup rerun correctness on config change

description: Established a functional tmux-rerun harness that changes `.tmux.conf` in a temp chezmoi source and checks whether the tmux setup script runs again; the current `run_onchange_after_40-setup-tmux.sh` scores 0 because config changes do not retrigger plugin/bootstrap setup.

Reverted the tmux setup script back to `run_after_40-setup-tmux.sh` and documented the dependency caveat in the ideas backlog so `.tmux.conf` plugin changes retrigger setup correctly again; the functional tmux-rerun harness improved from 0 to 100 and `chezmoi apply` still succeeds.

Re-established the 20x warm-apply baseline after restoring tmux correctness, so further process-count experiments compare against the current stable configuration rather than the brief unsafe tmux variant.

Converted the Ansible bootstrap to `run_onchange_after_` so routine warm applies no longer launch its no-op install probe every time; the script is a bootstrap-style installer with its own state marker, and the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 12.9s.

Tried converting fzf setup to a `run_onchange_` template so pin changes would retrigger installs, but the repo's template data does not expose an fzf pin (`.pinned.fzf` is absent), so the templating approach was invalid and not worth keeping in this performance loop.

Converted the Homebrew bootstrap to `run_onchange_after_` so routine warm applies stop launching a no-op OS/package probe every time; package-list edits still retrigger because they change the script itself, and the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 12.5s.

Tried replacing the summary's `find | wc` state count with a bash glob/array count, but against the current improved warm-apply baseline the 20x benchmark stayed within noise (12.6s/12.9s), so this micro-optimization is not worth keeping.

Converted fzf setup to `run_onchange_after_` as a template that inlines the pinned fzf version from `.chezmoiversion.toml`, so version pin changes still retrigger the script while routine warm applies skip its no-op version checks; the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 12.2s and a temp-source render check confirmed pin changes alter the rendered script content.

Converted the pinned Starship installer to `run_onchange_after_`, which is safe for version updates because the script is already templated by the pin and only needs to rerun when that rendered content changes; the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 11.9s.

Converted the pinned Codex installer to `run_onchange_after_` so routine warm applies stop launching another no-op version probe every time; because the script is templated by the codex pin, version updates still retrigger it, and the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 11.6s.

Converted the pinned Claude Code installer to `run_onchange_after_` so routine warm applies no longer start another no-op Node/npm check every time; the script remains pin-sensitive because its rendered content includes the pinned npm version, and the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 11.2s.

Converted the pinned Bun installer to `run_onchange_after_`, letting pin changes continue to retrigger through the templated script while routine warm applies skip another no-op tool-version probe; the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 10.9s.

Converted the pinned Jujutsu installer to `run_onchange_after_`, preserving automatic reruns on version-pin/template changes while removing another routine no-op setup process from warm applies; the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 10.5s.

Converted the pinned Neovim installer to `run_onchange_after_`, relying on the templated pinned version to retrigger the script when Neovim policy changes while removing another steady-state no-op installer from warm applies; the 20x warm-apply benchmark improved from 13.2s to 10.2s.

Converted the Node/NVM bootstrap to `run_onchange_after_`, which is safe for source-driven version policy changes because the rendered script already depends on the NVM and Node pins; routine warm applies now skip another steady-state NVM startup path, improving the 20x benchmark from 13.2s to 9.8s.

Converted the uv/Python bootstrap to `run_onchange_after_`, relying on the rendered script's pinned uv/Python versions to retrigger on source-policy changes while removing one of the most expensive steady-state no-op setup paths from warm applies; the 20x benchmark improved from 13.2s to 7.9s.
